content:
Players are permitted and encouraged to talk to one another during play, and to work as a team to plan and execute the best course of action, including discussing cards in play and in hand. Players are not obligated to disclose the cards in their hand.

While resolving an encounter card with the peril keyword, players are not permitted to consult with one another.

• If a game function or card ability is directed toward a game element, that element becomes the target of that function/ability for the duration of its resolution. Examples: the villain, a minion, an enemy, a scheme, a hero, an ally, a character, a player, you, a card.
  • If an ability/game function requires one or more targets, it can only be initiated if it has at least one valid target.
  • A target is valid if any part of that ability can affect that target; the cost of an ability is not considered when determining validity.
  • A target is not valid if the ability would cause the target to perform a game function another ability says it cannot perform.
  • Damage dealt but not taken (e.g. prevented) is still considered to affect a target.
  • A target 'cannot take damage' is not valid for an ability whose only effect is dealing it damage.
  • A target that cannot be attacked/thwarted is not valid for an attack/thwart-labeled ability.
  • An ability/game function targeting multiple elements of a type can be initiated as long as at least one is valid (it does not resolve against invalid ones).
  • An ability referring to a future target does not require a target to initiate; a search effect requires only a searchable game area.
Target threat is the amount of threat required for the main scheme deck to advance. It is located in the upper left corner of the card, before the title.

• The team-up keyword names two characters.
  • A player's chosen identity must match one of the named characters for a team-up card to be included in their deck.
  • A card with team-up cannot be played unless both named friendly characters (identity or ally) are in play. Equivalent constant ability restricts deckbuilding and play accordingly.
  • An ally counts as a named character if either its title or subtitle matches.

After a minion with teamwork enters play and engages a player, if there is at least one other minion sharing the specified trait in play, the minion that just entered play activates against the player it's engaged with.

  • Equivalent to:
    Forced Response: After this minion enters play, if there is another [Trait] minion in play, this minion activates against the engaged player.
  • If being revealed, teamwork resolves after any 'When Revealed' abilities.

A card with temporary must be discarded from play at the end of the round.

  • Equivalent to:
    Forced Interrupt: When the round ends, discard this card from play.

The text box of a card is the area containing the card's printed abilities, traits, and flavor text (if any). If a card ability references a card's 'text box,' that ability only references the printed abilities within it; icons printed within a card's text box are considered abilities within that text box.
See: Alteration Effect, Target.

If the effect text of an ability includes 'then', the text preceding 'then' must be fully true or resolved before the remainder can resolve. If the pre-'then' text fully resolves, the post-'then' text must also attempt to resolve; if it does not fully resolve, the post-'then' text does not attempt to resolve.
See: Alteration Effect.
Threat tokens are used to track the amount of threat on scheme cards.
See: Basic Power, Thwart.

• A hero or ally can use their basic thwart power to thwart a scheme (must exhaust; removes threat equal to THW value).
  • A character can only initiate a basic thwart if there is a scheme with at least one threat to remove or the character is confused.
  • If a triggered ability is labeled as a thwart (e.g. 'Hero Action (thwart)'), resolving it is considered to thwart the specified scheme; a hero does not exhaust unless specified.
  • An ability labeled as a thwart is a single thwart even removing multiple instances of threat.
See: Ability, Interrupt, Response, "Would".

Tough is a status that prevents a character from taking damage.

  • A tough status card resolves like:
    Forced Interrupt: When this character would take damage, remove a tough status card from it instead.
  • If a character with a tough status card would take any damage, prevent all of it and discard a tough status card instead (only one discarded per instance, even with multiple tough cards).
  • When a hero with a tough status card defends an attack, reduce damage by DEF first; if reduced to 0, the hero does not lose the tough status card.
  • A character prevented from taking damage by a tough status card is not considered to have taken damage.

When a character with the toughness keyword enters play, place a tough status card on it.

  • Equivalent to:
    Forced Response: After this character enters play, give it a tough status card.

Many cards have one or more traits listed at the top of the text box, printed in bold italics. Traits have no inherent effects on the game; some card abilities reference cards that possess or lack specific traits.

Traits are not considered part of a card's printed text box for the purpose of card abilities.

Treachery is an encounter card type representing tactics, tricks, disasters, and other immediate occurrences confronting players during a scenario.

  • If revealed, the revealing player must resolve its effects.
  • After resolving (or effects are canceled), place it in the encounter discard pile.
  • If a treachery causes one or more enemies to activate as its last effect, it is considered resolved and discarded after all those activations have resolved.
A triggered ability is indicated by a bold timing trigger followed by a colon and the rest of the ability text.

A triggering condition is a specific occurrence that takes place in the game; on card abilities, it indicates the timing point at which the ability may be used (usually following 'when' or 'after'). Each Interrupt and Response ability can only be triggered once per occurrence of its triggering condition (multiple copies of a card can each be triggered).

If a single game occurrence creates multiple triggering conditions, those conditions are handled with a single interrupt window and single response window, resolved in any order.

When a player is instructed to tuck a card under another card, they place the tucked card faceup under the other card. Tucked cards are not in play and are not considered 'attached' to the card under which they are tucked.

When a card leaves play, each card tucked under it is discarded.
